Title: Cellulose Synthases and Synthesis in Arabidopsis

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CesA6 promoter (used to express CesA2 or CesA5 cDNA in prc1-1; native/tissue-relevant promoter)
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Core cellulose synthase subunit for primary wall formation; engineering should preserve function because strong loss causes severe cellulose deficiency and lethality. Best used in precise allele engineering or phosphorylation-site tuning rather than knockout. Could be stacked with CesA3/CesA6-complex optimization for biomass traits, but major caution is developmental defects.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes primary wall cellulose synthesis; defective CesA1 reduces cellulose and causes radial swelling and growth defects.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Temperature-sensitive mutant rsw1; co-immunoprecipitation showing complex membership; phosphosite mutagenesis with phenotypic complementation and live-cell imaging of CesA particle movement.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Restrictive temperature reveals rsw1 phenotype; microtubule disruption alters phosphorylation-dependent movement behavior.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in tissues undergoing primary wall formation; part of primary wall CesA complex.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in tissues undergoing primary wall formation.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Plasma membrane phosphoproteomics (phosphorylation sites previously identified; no accession reported).
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CesA1 has unique non-redundant function in primary wall complex and null mutations are gametophytic lethal. Phosphorylation status affects bidirectional motility symmetry of CesA complexes along cortical microtubules.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss or severe impairment causes cellulose deficiency, dwarfism/radial swelling, and lethality at null state.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cellulose synthase catalytic subunit (GT2 family) with plant CesA conserved features.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plasma membrane; CesA complexes also trafficked via Golgi-associated compartments.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CesA1 is an essential catalytic subunit of the primary wall cellulose synthase complex. 2. Mutation in CesA1 (rsw1) lowers cellulose and causes radial swelling under restrictive temperature. 3. Phosphorylation of CesA1 regulates CesA complex motility and thereby cellulose deposition.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Primary and secondary cell walls; especially primary cell walls for CesA1-associated function
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Main load-bearing wall polymer; supports cell expansion, plant structure, and biomass/industrial fiber use.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential primary wall CesA component; useful only for conservative enhancement of cellulose deposition, not disruption. Candidate for biomass engineering in combination with other primary wall CesAs, with caution due to lethality/serious growth effects if compromised.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for primary wall cellulose synthesis as a unique subunit of the primary wall CesA complex.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Co-immunoprecipitation identifying CesA3 in the primary wall complex; genetic evidence and null mutant lethality.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not specifically reported beyond developmental primary-wall context.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CesA3 appears to have a unique function in the primary wall complex and cannot be substituted by CesA6-like isoforms.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CesA3 is a core catalytic component of the primary wall cellulose synthase complex. 2. It acts non-redundantly with CesA1. 3. Its loss is lethal, indicating indispensable contribution to cellulose deposition.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Structural wall polymer enabling anisotropic growth and mechanical strength.

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native CesA6 promoter used experimentally to express CesA2/CesA5 cDNAs for complementation context
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target to modulate primary wall cellulose in vegetative tissues; partial redundancy with CesA2/CesA5/CesA9 may buffer engineering. Gene stacking with related CesAs may improve biomass while reducing severe penalties, but incomplete interchangeability must be considered.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to primary wall cellulose synthesis; mutation causes reduced root and etiolated hypocotyl growth.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: prc1-1 mutant characterization; co-immunoprecipitation in primary wall complex; promoter-swap complementation with CesA2/CesA5.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Etiolated hypocotyl growth context; developmental/environmental specialization suggested.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Primary wall-forming tissues; partially overlapping with CesA2/CesA5/CesA9 and likely condition/development dependent.
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: CesA1; CesA3; CesA2; CesA5; CesA9; CSI1
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CesA6 is partially functionally redundant with CesA2 and CesA5; CesA9 may also be related, especially in floral development, but may have seed secondary-wall role as well.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Mutation reduces root and hypocotyl elongation; altered primary wall synthesis can impair growth.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plasma membrane and Golgi-associated CesA trafficking compartments.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CesA6 is a major component of the primary wall cellulose synthase complex. 2. Loss of CesA6 causes mild cellulose-related growth defects because CesA2 and CesA5 partially compensate. 3. CesA6-related isoforms are not fully interchangeable, implying specialized functions.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Redundancy-supporting CesA for primary wall engineering; could be stacked with CesA6/CesA5 to stabilize cellulose synthesis in specific tissues. However, limited functional equivalence to CesA6 means promoter and tissue context matter.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Partially redundant contributor to primary wall cellulose synthesis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic redundancy analysis; expression of CesA2 cDNA under CesA6 promoter in prc1-1 only partially restored phenotype.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: May function under specific developmental or environmental conditions.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Partially overlapping with other CesA6-related genes.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Partially overlapping with other CesA6-related genes.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Phosphoproteomics detected sites S715; T718 (no accession reported).
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CesA2 belongs to the CesA6-related subgroup and is only partially functionally redundant with CesA6.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely growth and wall deposition defects when redundancy is insufficient.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CesA2 is a CesA6-related subunit associated with primary wall cellulose synthesis. 2. It can partially compensate for CesA6 loss. 3. Distinct expression and incomplete complementation indicate specialized roles.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Mechanical support and growth-related wall deposition.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Accessory primary-wall CesA candidate for tissue-specific cellulose enhancement. Can be considered in gene-stacking with CesA6-related isoforms, but incomplete redundancy and context dependence require careful deployment.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Partially redundant contributor to primary wall cellulose synthesis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic redundancy analysis; expression of CesA5 cDNA under CesA6 promoter in prc1-1 only partially restored phenotype.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Partially overlapping with other CesA6-related isoforms.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Partially overlapping with other CesA6-related isoforms.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Phosphoproteomics detected sites S229; S230 (no accession reported).
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CesA5 is CesA6-related and only partially functionally redundant with CesA6.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential wall/growth defects when compensation fails.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CesA5 is associated with the primary wall CesA machinery. 2. It partially compensates for CesA6 deficiency. 3. Incomplete complementation suggests specialized non-identical function.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Structural cell wall polymer for growth.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential tissue-specific cellulose engineering target, especially floral or seed coat wall traits. Should be manipulated in tissue-specific manner because role may switch between primary-wall redundancy and seed secondary-wall deposition.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Genetic evidence suggests redundancy with CesA6-related CesAs, especially during floral development; additionally has a non-redundant role in seed coat secondary wall thickening where mutant seeds have ~25% less cellulose.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic evidence in CesA6-related group; mutant seed phenotype analysis in cesa9 with developmental timing of defects.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Seed coat phenotype evident around 10 days after pollination during secondary wall deposition.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Likely floral development and seed coat contexts; may vary by tissue.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Likely floral development and seed coat contexts.
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Potentially CesA6-related CesAs; secondary wall CesA partners not confirmed
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Role remains ambiguous: may function with primary wall CesA6-related isoforms and/or in a seed-specific secondary wall complex. Membership in either or both complexes remains unresolved.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Mutation disturbs seed coat epidermal cell shapes and reduces cellulose; misengineering may alter reproduction or seed traits.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CesA9 is related to CesA6-like isoforms but its exact complex assignment is unresolved. 2. It contributes to floral development-related cellulose synthesis and has a clear role in seed coat secondary wall thickening. 3. cesa9 mutants show reduced seed cellulose and abnormal epidermal cell shape.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Seed coat secondary walls; possibly primary walls in floral tissues
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Wall strengthening, seed coat structure, and plant biomass.

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: 35S/overexpression not specified in review for Arabidopsis; cotton SuSy overexpressed in poplar in cited work
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Carbon-supply target for cellulose enhancement; isoform selection is critical because some SuSy isoforms increase cellulose while others do not. Best deployed with tissue-specific expression in sink tissues or woody biomass crops. Caution: Arabidopsis quadruple sus mutant shows cellulose can be maintained without most SuSy activity, so effect is context-dependent.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes UDP-glucose supply for cellulose biosynthesis, but is not strictly required in Arabidopsis because alternative pathways can compensate.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Localization of membrane-bound forms; in vitro association with glucan synthase-active fractions; transgenic overexpression in poplar; Arabidopsis quadruple sus1 sus2 sus3 sus4 mutant analysis.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Sink versus source tissue context; non-photosynthetic cells may rely more on alternative invertase route when SuSy is absent.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Mainly in sink tissues according to cited evidence.
Common_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: sus1; sus2; sus3; sus4 not individually specified
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Cellulose synthase machinery (association proposed); CesA complexes not definitively established
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: SuSy may act at plasma membrane/cell wall and was found in active detergent-soluble fractions from azuki bean; however Arabidopsis quadruple sus mutant retains normal cellulose, indicating non-essential or compensated role. Isoform specificity is important.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: UGPase or invertase may compensate; overexpression effects differ by isoform and species; biomass benefit may be context specific.
Common_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis (and comparative evidence from cotton, mung bean, azuki bean, poplar, pea)
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytosol; plasma membrane-bound forms; cell wall-associated fractions.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SuSy can generate UDP-glucose for cellulose synthesis and localize near cellulose-producing sites. 2. Overexpression of certain SuSy isoforms can increase cellulose in poplar. 3. Arabidopsis sus1 sus2 sus3 sus4 retains normal cellulose, showing SuSy is not obligatorily required for cellulose biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Structural polymer and industrial biomass feedstock.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential precursor-supply target to raise UDP-glucose and cellulose, but evidence is mixed across species and growth penalties may occur. Use moderate/tissue-specific enhancement rather than constitutive strong overexpression.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies UDP-glucose for cellulose biosynthesis; ugp1 ugp2 mutants show growth defects and reduced CesA transcript abundance, suggesting contribution to cellulose-related metabolism.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: ugp1 ugp2 double mutant analysis; transgenic overexpression studies in poplar and tobacco.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Source versus sink tissue context may affect relative contribution.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Likely active broadly in source tissues and alongside SuSy in source tissues.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Likely active broadly in source tissues.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct effect on cellulose remains uncertain because cell-wall composition was not measured in Arabidopsis ugp1 ugp2 study; poplar overexpression increased cellulose but reduced growth, whereas tobacco overexpression had no effect.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible growth penalty when overexpressed; effect on cellulose may be species dependent.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UGPase synthesizes UDP-glucose, a substrate for cellulose polymerization. 2. Arabidopsis ugp1 ugp2 mutants show growth defects and reduced CesA transcript levels. 3. Engineering evidence is inconsistent across species, leaving its quantitative role in cellulose deposition unresolved.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Indirect carbon-allocation target for non-photosynthetic tissues; may support wall synthesis under sink conditions. Requires caution because evidence for direct cellulose impact is incomplete and broad sugar-metabolism perturbation can impair growth.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely supports carbon supply from sucrose hydrolysis to non-photosynthetic cells; inv1 inv2 double mutants show reduced growth and strong root-growth defects, possibly limiting substrate for wall synthesis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: inv1 inv2 double mutant phenotyping and sugar-rescue experiments.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Non-photosynthetic cell context; exogenous sugar addition partially rescues root growth.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No direct cell wall composition measurements were reported, so link to cellulose is inferential rather than definitive.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Mutants are smaller and have strong root growth defects; manipulation may broadly affect carbon metabolism.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. INV1 and INV2 likely provide carbon from sucrose in non-photosynthetic cells. 2. Double mutants have severe root-growth defects partially rescued by sugars. 3. Their connection to cellulose synthesis is proposed but not directly demonstrated by wall analysis.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cell wall of non-photosynthetic tissues, especially roots
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Structural wall polymer required for elongation and integrity.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising accessory factor for improving cellulose deposition or biomass; likely better suited for moderate enhancement rather than knockout. Could be combined with CesA engineering to improve complex guidance/delivery. Caution: exact mechanism remains unresolved.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes cellulose accumulation and normal CesA complex activity; csi1 mutants have reduced cellulose and decreased cortical CesA activity.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Co-expression analysis; yeast two-hybrid interaction with CesA1 and CesA6; mutant cellulose measurement and live imaging of CesA activity.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Co-expressed with primary wall CesA genes.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Co-expressed with primary wall CesA genes.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Likely non-enzymatic scaffold with Armadillo repeats and C2 domain; may link CesA complexes to microtubules or assist delivery to plasma membrane.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss reduces cellulose and CesA activity; mechanism uncertainty complicates engineering.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Plant-specific protein with 10–20 Armadillo repeats and a C-terminal C2 domain.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CSI1 interacts with primary wall CesA components and is co-expressed with them. 2. csi1 mutants have lower cellulose and reduced CesA complex activity. 3. CSI1 likely acts as a scaffold involved in CesA guidance along microtubules and/or complex delivery to the plasma membrane.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Mechanical support and directional cell expansion.

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: RNAi silencing used experimentally; promoter not specified
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential secondary-wall associated target for vessel formation/wood quality engineering, but current evidence is insufficient for direct deployment because biochemical function remains unknown.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with secondary wall synthesis and tracheary element differentiation; down-regulation delays differentiation and affects root vessel element formation.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Induction in xylem-vessel differentiation systems; protein interaction (TED6 with IRX3/CesA7); RNAi silencing phenotypes.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced during tracheary element differentiation, similar to secondary wall CesAs.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Induced during tracheary element differentiation, similar to secondary wall CesAs.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Both are type I membrane proteins of unknown function; exact role in cellulose production remains unclear.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Silencing delays vascular differentiation and alters vessel formation.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Type I membrane proteins of unknown function.
Common_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis (with induction evidence from Zinnia elegans cultures)
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TED6 and TED7 are induced with secondary wall synthesis programs. 2. TED6 interacts with secondary wall CesA7. 3. Reducing TED6/TED7 delays tracheary differentiation and perturbs vessel element formation, implicating them in secondary wall cellulose-related development.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Secondary cell wall in vascular tissues
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Secondary wall strength and vascular integrity.

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Overexpression of aspen homolog in Arabidopsis referenced; promoter not specified
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate to improve cellulose quantity/quality and perhaps microfibril properties, but mechanism is unresolved. Better for cautious modulation than strong perturbation; may be useful in biomass crops if growth penalties can be avoided.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for both primary and secondary wall cellulose production; mutations strongly reduce cellulose and lower CesA complex velocity.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis in primary and secondary walls; measurement of cellulose content; in muro cellulase activity upon overexpression of aspen homolog; live imaging of CesA velocity.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not specified; functions in primary and secondary wall-producing tissues.
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Association with CesA complex suggested in Populus co-IP, but not directly confirmed in Arabidopsis
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Encodes putative membrane-bound endo-β-(1,4)-glucanase. Proposed roles include releasing newly synthesized microfibrils, removing primer, or modifying crystallinity.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Mutations cause major cellulose reduction and growth defects.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Putative endo-β-(1,4)-glucanase with single transmembrane domain.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. KOR is essential for normal cellulose deposition in primary and secondary walls. 2. kor mutants have reduced cellulose and slower primary wall CesA complex movement. 3. KOR likely acts during cellulose chain processing or microfibril assembly rather than as a core CesA catalytic subunit.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Wall strength, anisotropic growth, vascular integrity.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Good target for improving cellulose microfibril organization and anisotropic growth rather than simply increasing cellulose amount. Tissue-specific manipulation may improve fiber quality; caution due to root/hypocotyl growth defects in mutants.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Affects crystalline cellulose deposition and microfibril orientation, especially during rapid cell elongation.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotyping in roots/hypocotyls; measurement of crystalline cellulose in root elongation zone; analysis of microfibril orientation.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not specified; active in root elongation zone/hypocotyl elongation-related tissues.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: GPI-anchored extracellular plasma membrane protein; no enzymatic activity assigned. Likely influences cellulose microfibril deposition/orientation.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Mutants show abnormal anisotropic expansion in roots and hypocotyls.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. COB is required for proper anisotropic growth. 2. cob mutants have less crystalline cellulose in the root elongation zone. 3. COB primarily affects microfibril deposition/orientation during rapid elongation.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Primary cell wall, especially elongating root/hypocotyl tissues
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports directional expansion and wall strength.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for secondary wall biomass/fiber improvement, but direct mechanism remains unclear. Suitable for cautious manipulation in vascular tissues only.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Affects cellulose content in secondary cell walls.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant studies in Arabidopsis and rice cited in review.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Member of COBRA-like family associated with secondary wall cellulose synthesis.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential wall integrity defects if disrupted.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Likely extracellular side of plasma membrane
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. COBL4 is a COBRA-like factor affecting secondary wall cellulose content. 2. Evidence from Arabidopsis and rice supports a conserved role in wall formation. 3. It likely modulates cellulose deposition rather than catalyzing synthesis.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Root hair and architecture target related to cellulose deposition, but not a primary biomass-engineering candidate due to developmental specificity and uncertain mechanism.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Affects root architecture and causes swollen, short root hairs, likely through compromised cell wall deposition.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Phenotype implies association with cellulose-related wall deposition but direct biochemical link is limited.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altered root architecture and root hair morphology.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Likely extracellular side of plasma membrane
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. COBL9 affects root hair morphology and root architecture. 2. The phenotype is consistent with altered wall deposition. 3. Its role is likely developmental and cellulose-associated rather than directly catalytic.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cell wall structure supporting polarized growth.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important supporting factor for cellulose deposition, but poor current engineering candidate because biochemical activity is unresolved and stress/sugar responses complicate deployment.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for normal cellulose accumulation and cell expansion; mutants show incomplete cell walls, strong cellulose reduction, root swelling, and short etiolated hypocotyls.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant characterization; cellulose quantification; heterologous expression assays failing to detect chitinase activity.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Mutant root development is sensitive to high nitrate, sucrose, and chloride conditions; etiolated hypocotyl context noted.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not specified; function evident in roots and etiolated hypocotyls.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Chitinase-like protein without confirmed chitinase activity; plants do not normally produce chitin. Mechanistic role in cellulose deposition remains unclear.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Mutants have severe growth defects and stress-sensitive root development.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Not clearly specified in this review
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CTL1/POM1 is required for proper cell wall formation and cellulose accumulation. 2. Mutants display root swelling, reduced hypocotyl elongation, and incomplete cell walls. 3. Although chitinase-like by sequence, its biochemical role in cellulose production remains unresolved.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Primary cell walls of roots and hypocotyls

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential leverage point for cellulose production, but unsuitable for direct broad engineering until mechanism and localization are clarified. Could be explored with tissue-specific modulation.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential for normal cellulose production; mutants are sterile, dwarfed, and have ~33% less crystalline cellulose.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Associated with ABA and high sugar response through abi8 allele.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Plant-specific protein with no homology to known-function proteins; reported subcellular localization is inconsistent (plasma membrane/internal structures, cell wall, or cytoplasmic puncta). Also linked to ABA and sugar signaling.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong developmental defects, sterility, dwarfism; possible altered ABA/sugar responses.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Reported variously as plasma membrane/internal structures; cell wall; cytoplasmic puncta
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. KOB1 is essential for normal cellulose accumulation. 2. kob1 mutants are sterile, dwarfed, and cellulose-deficient. 3. The molecular mechanism and localization of KOB1 remain unresolved, with possible links to ABA and sugar signaling.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Primary catalytic target for biomass/cell wall engineering, but essentiality demands fine-tuning rather than disruption. Phosphosite editing may alter deposition pattern without full loss of function.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains that assemble into cellulose microfibrils.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for primary wall cellulose deposition; mutation reduces cellulose and causes rsw1 swelling phenotype.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Temperature-sensitive mutant analysis; co-immunoprecipitation; phosphosite mutagenesis and complementation; live-cell imaging.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Restrictive temperature; microtubule disruption affects phospho-dependent movement phenotype.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Catalytic subunit in rosette cellulose synthase complex; phosphorylation modulates complex motility.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Severe cellulose deficiency and lethality when strongly impaired.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step within cellulose synthase complex, but formal rate-limiting status not established
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CesA1 directly polymerizes cellulose in the primary wall complex. 2. rsw1 demonstrates its requirement for cellulose deposition. 3. Phosphorylation regulates movement behavior of cellulose synthase complexes.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Load-bearing wall polymer and industrial biomass precursor.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential catalytic target for cellulose enhancement only via subtle upregulation or allele optimization.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains in the primary wall cellulose synthase complex.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Indispensable for primary wall cellulose synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step not formally established as rate limiting
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CesA3 is a direct cellulose-polymerizing subunit in primary walls. 2. It functions non-redundantly with CesA1. 3. Loss of function is lethal, underscoring essential catalytic role.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native CesA6 promoter used in complementation experiments
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Good primary-wall catalytic target for moderate enhancement and combinatorial engineering with CesA2/CesA5; redundancy provides some buffering.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains as part of the primary wall CesA complex.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes primary wall cellulose synthesis; mutation reduces elongation growth.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Etiolated hypocotyl/root growth; developmental specialization possible.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Primary wall-forming tissues with partial overlap to CesA2/CesA5.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Partially redundant with CesA2/CesA5 and perhaps CesA9.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Defects impair root and hypocotyl elongation.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key catalytic component; rate-limiting status not established
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CesA6 directly contributes to cellulose polymerization in primary walls. 2. Mutants show growth defects due to reduced cellulose synthesis. 3. Related CesA isoforms can only partially substitute for it.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Auxiliary primary-wall catalytic subunit for tissue-specific gene stacking.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains as a CesA6-related catalytic subunit.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports primary wall cellulose biosynthesis through partial redundancy with CesA6.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Insufficient compensation may reduce wall synthesis/growth.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CesA2 is a catalytic CesA6-related isoform in primary wall synthesis. 2. It partially replaces CesA6. 3. Specialized expression/function prevents full redundancy.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Auxiliary catalytic subunit for primary-wall engineering under tissue-specific control.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains as a CesA6-related primary-wall enzyme.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports primary wall cellulose synthesis with partial redundancy to CesA6.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CesA5 is a catalytic CesA6-related primary wall subunit. 2. It partially compensates for CesA6. 3. It is not functionally identical to CesA6.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential tissue-specific seed coat or floral wall engineering target.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Likely polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains in a tissue-specific CesA complex, especially in seed coat secondary walls.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to seed coat secondary wall cellulose deposition and possibly primary-wall related functions in floral tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant seed coat phenotyping and developmental timing evidence.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Seed secondary wall deposition around 10 DAP.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Complex assignment remains unresolved between primary and secondary wall systems.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Seed coat secondary wall cellulose synthesis (putative); possibly primary-wall related branch
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Mutation disrupts seed coat cell shape and lowers cellulose.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CesA9 is a catalytic cellulose synthase with tissue-specific role. 2. It is important for seed coat secondary wall cellulose deposition. 3. Whether it also participates in primary wall complexes remains unresolved.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Wall strengthening and seed coat structure.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Core secondary-wall catalytic target for wood/stem biomass improvement; must be maintained because subunits are non-redundant.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains in the secondary wall cellulose synthase complex.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential for secondary wall cellulose synthesis; mutants have collapsed xylem and ~70% lower stem cellulose.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: irx5 mutant characterization; genetic analysis; interaction studies among secondary-wall CesAs.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: All secondary wall CesA subunits appear non-redundant; CesA4 may uniquely form homodimers and link complexes in rosette structure.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss causes severe xylem collapse and likely major biomass penalties.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CesA4/IRX5 is an essential catalytic component of the secondary wall cellulose synthase complex. 2. irx mutants have collapsed xylem and strongly reduced cellulose. 3. CesA4 may contribute a unique structural interaction role through homodimerization.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential target for secondary-wall biomass engineering, but non-redundancy requires maintenance of function.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains in the secondary wall cellulose synthase complex.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential for xylem secondary wall cellulose synthesis; mutants have collapsed xylem and major cellulose reduction.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: irx3 mutant characterization; split-ubiquitin/BiFC interaction assays; TED6 interaction evidence.
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Two phosphorylation sites detected in hypervariable region; proposed to control proteasome-dependent degradation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Can interact with other secondary wall CesAs; degradation may be phospho-regulated.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CesA7/IRX3 is a catalytic secondary-wall CesA required for xylem cellulose deposition. 2. It interacts with other secondary-wall CesAs and TED6. 3. Phosphorylation may regulate its degradation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Mechanical support and vascular transport integrity.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential target for increasing secondary wall biomass in stems/wood, but requires careful tuning to avoid xylem collapse.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains in the secondary wall cellulose synthase complex.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential for secondary wall cellulose synthesis; mutants show collapsed xylem and reduced cellulose.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: irx1 mutant characterization; interaction studies with other secondary wall CesAs.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Non-redundant with CesA4 and CesA7 in xylem vessels.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong vascular and growth defects if impaired.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CesA8/IRX1 is a catalytic secondary-wall CesA required for cellulose deposition in xylem. 2. Mutants have irx phenotype and much lower cellulose. 3. It functions non-redundantly with CesA4 and CesA7.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Overexpression in poplar/tobacco discussed; promoter not specified in review
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential precursor-pool engineering target for cellulose increase, but with species/isoform specificity and uncertain necessity in Arabidopsis.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts sucrose and UDP into UDP-glucose and fructose, supplying activated glucose donor for cellulose synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports UDP-glucose supply for cellulose synthesis, though not obligatorily required in Arabidopsis due to compensatory pathways.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical localization/association studies; overexpression studies; quadruple sus mutant analysis.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Sink-tissue emphasis; non-photosynthetic versus source tissue differences.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: sus1; sus2; sus3; sus4 not specified further
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Associated with cellulose synthase machinery in some preparations
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Isoform-dependent effects; direct integration into CesA machinery remains debated.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression effects vary; not always increases cellulose; Arabidopsis compensation can mask role.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis (comparative evidence includes cotton, mung bean, azuki bean)
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytosol; plasma membrane-associated forms; cell wall-associated fractions
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SuSy can supply UDP-glucose for cellulose synthesis near the plasma membrane. 2. Some SuSy overexpression events increase cellulose. 3. Arabidopsis sus mutants show that SuSy is dispensable under some conditions due to compensatory metabolism.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Possible upstream engineering node for cellulose increase, but strong overexpression may penalize growth and outcomes are species dependent.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts glucose-1-phosphate and UTP into UDP-glucose and pyrophosphate, supplying activated glucose for cellulose synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to UDP-glucose supply associated with growth and CesA transcript maintenance.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: ugp1 ugp2 mutant analysis; overexpression studies in poplar and tobacco.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Likely broad cytosolic expression, especially in source tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct cellulose effects remain uncertain because cell wall composition was not measured in key Arabidopsis mutant study.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Growth penalty observed in poplar overexpression; no effect in tobacco.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UGPase produces UDP-glucose for cellulose synthase. 2. Arabidopsis ugp1 ugp2 mutants show growth defects and lower CesA transcript levels. 3. Engineering outcomes differ by species, so its control over cellulose flux remains unresolved.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Sucrose catabolism supporting cellulose precursor supply
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential carbon-allocation target in roots/non-photosynthetic tissues; only indirect evidence links it to cellulose, so deployment should await wall-composition validation.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes sucrose into glucose and fructose, providing substrates that can feed UDP-glucose synthesis and wall biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely supports substrate availability for cell wall synthesis in non-photosynthetic cells.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: inv1 inv2 double mutant and sugar-rescue experiments.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Non-photosynthetic root tissues; exogenous sugar rescue.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct wall compositional evidence is lacking.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced plant size and root growth in double mutant.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. INV1/INV2 hydrolyze sucrose to provide carbon skeletons for wall synthesis. 2. Double mutants have strong root-growth defects. 3. Their importance for cellulose is proposed but not directly confirmed by wall analysis.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to alter cellulose assembly or crystallinity in biomass crops, but mechanism unresolved and severe penalties possible if strongly perturbed.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Likely hydrolyzes or trims β-1,4-glucan during cellulose synthesis/assembly, possibly releasing nascent chains, removing primers, or modulating crystallinity.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential for normal cellulose deposition in primary and secondary walls.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant cellulose phenotyping; reduced CesA velocity; overexpression of homolog increasing cellulase activity.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Association with CesA complexes proposed in Populus xylem co-IP
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Exact catalytic role during cellulose deposition remains unresolved.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Severe cellulose loss and growth defects upon mutation.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important accessory step, not established as rate limiting
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. KOR is needed for full cellulose accumulation in both primary and secondary walls. 2. It influences CesA complex velocity. 3. It likely functions in nascent cellulose processing or microfibril assembly rather than core polymerization.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Wall strength and ordered microfibril assembly.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Top-tier target for secondary wall engineering and biomass improvement because it sits high in the transcriptional hierarchy. Best used with tissue-specific promoters to avoid ectopic wall deposition and growth penalties.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts as a master positive regulator of secondary wall formation, indirectly promoting cellulose accumulation via activation of downstream MYB regulators and wall-biosynthetic genes.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Induction/cell culture systems; global target identification study.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Master regulator of the secondary cell wall program.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Secondary wall differentiation context in vascular tissues/cell cultures.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Secondary wall-forming tissues; xylem/fiber differentiation contexts.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Likely feedforward through activation of downstream MYB TFs.
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Downstream MYB transcription factors (group, not individually specified here)
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Secondary wall biosynthesis and modification program
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Global identification of target genes (no accession reported).
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct targets include many secondary wall genes, though secondary wall CesAs were not identified as direct targets in cited study.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely risk of ectopic lignification/secondary wall deposition and growth defects if misexpressed.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Secondary wall-associated genes; MYB transcription factors; xylan biosynthetic genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates downstream secondary wall regulators and biosynthetic genes.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SND1 is a core NAC master regulator of secondary wall formation. 2. It activates broad downstream wall synthesis programs, including genes for hemicellulose and other secondary wall functions. 3. It likely influences cellulose synthesis indirectly through lower-tier TFs.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Secondary cell walls of vascular/fiber tissues

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for engineering vessel-specific secondary wall biosynthesis and biomass, preferably with cell-type-specific promoters.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Master positive regulator of vessel secondary wall formation, indirectly supporting cellulose deposition.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cell culture induction systems; global target identification.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Master regulator of vessel-associated secondary wall program.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Developing vessel elements/secondary wall differentiation contexts.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Likely feedforward through MYB and secondary-wall gene network.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Secondary wall biosynthesis/modification and programmed cell death
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Global target identification study (no accession reported).
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct targets included many secondary wall and programmed cell death genes, but not secondary wall CesAs in cited report.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Misexpression may trigger ectopic vessel differentiation or cell death programs.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Secondary wall-associated genes; xylan biosynthetic genes; downstream MYB TFs
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VND7 is a master regulator of vessel secondary wall formation. 2. It controls broad secondary wall and programmed cell death networks. 3. Its effect on cellulose is mainly indirect through the secondary wall transcriptional hierarchy.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Vascular support and water transport integrity.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for vessel wall engineering, but likely should be deployed in vascular-specific contexts.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Master regulator of secondary wall formation in vessels, contributing indirectly to cellulose accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cell culture induction studies cited in review.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Core NAC master regulator of vessel secondary walls.
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Downstream secondary wall TFs not individually specified
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Named as part of core NAC master regulator set.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely ectopic wall deposition if misexpressed.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Secondary wall regulators and biosynthetic genes (not detailed in review)
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VND6 belongs to the core NAC master regulators of secondary wall formation. 2. It promotes vessel differentiation-associated wall biosynthesis. 3. Its effect on cellulose is through activation of downstream transcriptional networks.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for strengthening secondary walls in fibers and related tissues, best via tissue-specific expression.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Master positive regulator of secondary wall thickening, indirectly enhancing cellulose deposition.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Secondary wall induction studies cited in review.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Core master regulator of secondary wall thickening.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Likely feedforward via downstream MYB TFs.
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Downstream MYB TFs not individually specified
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Listed among core NAC master regulators for secondary wall formation.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential ectopic wall thickening or reduced growth if broadly misexpressed.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: MYB TFs and wall biosynthetic genes (group-level)
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NST1 is a core NAC regulator promoting secondary wall thickening. 2. It controls downstream TFs and wall biosynthetic programs. 3. Its cellulose effect is indirect but decisive at the developmental-program level.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Developmental cues for secondary wall thickening

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Companion target with NST1 for wall-thickening engineering in fiber tissues; use tissue-specific control.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Master regulator of secondary wall thickening, indirectly promoting cellulose deposition.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Secondary wall induction studies cited in review.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Core master regulator of secondary wall thickening.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Likely feedforward via downstream MYB TFs.
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Downstream MYB TFs not individually specified
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Listed among core NAC master regulators for secondary wall formation.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential ectopic secondary wall deposition if misexpressed.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: MYB TFs and wall biosynthetic genes (group-level)
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NST2 is a core NAC regulator of secondary wall thickening. 2. It acts high in the transcriptional hierarchy. 3. It indirectly determines cellulose accumulation through activation of wall biosynthetic programs.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Developmental cues for secondary wall deposition

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Site-directed mutagenesis of native protein; promoter not specified
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Excellent precision-engineering target: phosphosite editing can tune cellulose deposition and microfibril pattern without eliminating catalytic function. Best for fine control of wall architecture. Requires caution because certain phosphomimic/non-phosphorylatable variants fail to complement rsw1.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Phosphorylation state regulates CesA complex motility and thereby cellulose deposition pattern/efficiency.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Site-directed phosphosite mutagenesis, complementation of rsw1, live-cell imaging, microtubule disruption tests.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller of cellulose synthase complex behavior at the plasma membrane.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Microtubule integrity; restrictive temperature in rsw1 background.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is post-translational regulation of CesA1 rather than a separate gene product, but it is a decisive regulatory layer emphasized by the paper.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Incorrect phosphosite states impair growth and fail to rescue cellulose-deficient mutant.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Acts within primary wall CesA complex
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Alters CesA complex behavior rather than gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CesA1 phosphorylation controls the bidirectional motility symmetry of cellulose synthase complexes. 2. Specific phosphosite mutations impair complementation of rsw1 and alter complex movement. 3. This regulatory layer links cellulose deposition to microtubule-dependent behavior.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Load-bearing wall polymer with ordered deposition patterns.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Unknown kinase pathways; possibly receptor-like kinase-mediated signaling and microtubule context

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential upstream signaling target for reinforcing cellulose-defect response pathways and wall integrity signaling, but pathway is unresolved and pleiotropy risk is high.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Affects cellulose production/signaling associated with cellulose deficiency.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant studies cited in review (details not elaborated in review text).
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Potential upstream controller of cellulose-production-related signaling, but not yet mechanistically established.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Cell wall integrity signaling affecting cellulose production
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper explicitly states signaling network and control of cellulose production are not yet known.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely pleiotropic growth and stress-response effects if manipulated.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. THESEUS1 is a receptor-like kinase affecting cellulose production. 2. It is a candidate upstream regulator of cellulose-related phosphorylation/signaling. 3. Its precise signaling network remains unknown.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential wall-integrity signaling target, but not yet actionable due to unknown pathway and likely pleiotropy.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Affects cellulose production/signaling associated with cellulose-related processes.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant studies cited in review (details not elaborated).
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Potential condition-dependent signaling regulator of cellulose synthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Possibly feedback signaling from wall status.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Cell wall integrity signaling affecting cellulose production
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Underlying signaling network is not yet known according to review.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FEI1 is a receptor-like kinase affecting cellulose production. 2. It is proposed as a candidate upstream regulator of cellulose-associated phosphorylation/signaling. 3. The mechanistic pathway remains unresolved.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential wall-integrity signaling target, but currently too uncertain for direct engineering use.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Affects cellulose production/signaling associated with cellulose-related processes.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant studies cited in review (details not elaborated).
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Potential signaling regulator, but decisive role not mechanistically proven.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Possibly feedback signaling from wall status.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Cell wall integrity signaling affecting cellulose production
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FEI2 is a receptor-like kinase affecting cellulose production. 2. It is considered a candidate upstream regulator of cellulose-related control. 3. Its signaling mechanism remains unknown.
